title: Provision AWS Infrastructure with Terraform
summary: Terraform setup for AWS — install CLI, provider and backend config, S3 remote state, core resources (VPC, EC2, RDS, S3), variables/outputs, and CI/CD-friendly workspace strategy.
declaration: I will set up Terraform to provision AWS infrastructure. This covers installing Terraform, configuring the AWS provider, setting up remote state in S3, and creating core resources. If you need a different cloud provider (GCP, Azure) or a Terraform wrapper (Terragrunt, OpenTofu), say so before I begin.
instructions:
## Provision AWS Infrastructure with Terraform

### 1. Pre-flight checks
- Confirm the AWS CLI is installed: `aws --version`. If not, install from https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/install-cliv2.html.
- Confirm AWS credentials are configured: `aws sts get-caller-identity`. If not, run `aws configure` with an IAM user that has programmatic access.
- NEVER use the AWS root account credentials. Create an IAM user or role with least-privilege permissions.
- Confirm Terraform is NOT yet installed by checking `terraform version`. If it is, note the version — Terraform 1.5+ is required.

### 2. Install Terraform
- **macOS:** `brew tap hashicorp/tap && brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform`
- **Linux (Ubuntu/Debian):**
  ```bash
  wget -O- https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg
  echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list
  sudo apt update && sudo apt install terraform
  ```
- **Windows:** Download the zip from https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/downloads and add to PATH.
- Verify: `terraform version` should output `Terraform v1.x.x`.
- Install `tfenv` (`brew install tfenv`) if you need to switch Terraform versions across projects.

### 3. Initialize the project directory
- Create a directory for your infrastructure: `mkdir infra && cd infra`.
- Terraform files use the `.tf` extension. Create `main.tf`, `variables.tf`, `outputs.tf`, and `versions.tf`.
- Recommended directory structure:
  ```
  infra/
  ├── main.tf         # resource definitions
  ├── variables.tf    # input variables
  ├── outputs.tf      # output values
  ├── versions.tf     # provider version pins
  ├── terraform.tfvars  # variable values (gitignored)
  └── .terraform.lock.hcl  # lock file (commit this)
  ```

### 4. Configure provider versions (`versions.tf`)
  ```hcl
  terraform {
    required_version = ">= 1.5.0"

    required_providers {
      aws = {
        source  = "hashicorp/aws"
        version = "~> 5.0"
      }
    }
  }

  provider "aws" {
    region = var.aws_region

    default_tags {
      tags = {
        Project     = var.project_name
        Environment = var.environment
        ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      }
    }
  }
  ```
- Pin versions with `~>` (pessimistic constraint) to avoid breaking changes from minor updates.
- `default_tags` applies tags to every AWS resource automatically — use it to track costs.

### 5. Define variables (`variables.tf`)
  ```hcl
  variable "aws_region" {
    description = "AWS region for all resources"
    type        = string
    default     = "us-east-1"
  }

  variable "project_name" {
    description = "Project name used for resource naming and tagging"
    type        = string
  }

  variable "environment" {
    description = "Deployment environment"
    type        = string
    validation {
      condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "production"], var.environment)
      error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or production."
    }
  }

  variable "db_password" {
    description = "RDS master password"
    type        = string
    sensitive   = true
  }
  ```
- Mark secrets with `sensitive = true` — Terraform will redact them from plan output.
- Create `terraform.tfvars` (NEVER commit this file):
  ```hcl
  project_name = "myapp"
  environment  = "dev"
  db_password  = "changeme_supersecret"
  ```
- Add `terraform.tfvars` to `.gitignore`.

### 6. Set up S3 remote state backend
- Create the S3 bucket and DynamoDB table for state BEFORE adding the backend config (chicken-and-egg problem):
  ```bash
  aws s3api create-bucket \
    --bucket myapp-terraform-state \
    --region us-east-1

  aws s3api put-bucket-versioning \
    --bucket myapp-terraform-state \
    --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled

  aws dynamodb create-table \
    --table-name myapp-terraform-locks \
    --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \
    --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \
    --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST \
    --region us-east-1
  ```
- Add the backend to `versions.tf`:
  ```hcl
  terraform {
    backend "s3" {
      bucket         = "myapp-terraform-state"
      key            = "infra/terraform.tfstate"
      region         = "us-east-1"
      dynamodb_table = "myapp-terraform-locks"
      encrypt        = true
    }
  }
  ```
- Run `terraform init` to migrate local state to S3.
- Use unique bucket names — S3 bucket names are globally unique.

### 7. Create core resources (`main.tf`)
- **VPC with public/private subnets:**
  ```hcl
  resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
    cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
    enable_dns_hostnames = true
    enable_dns_support   = true
  }

  resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
    count             = 2
    vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
    cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(aws_vpc.main.cidr_block, 8, count.index)
    availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
    map_public_ip_on_launch = true
  }

  data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
    state = "available"
  }
  ```
- **RDS PostgreSQL:**
  ```hcl
  resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
    identifier        = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-db"
    engine            = "postgres"
    engine_version    = "16"
    instance_class    = "db.t3.micro"
    allocated_storage = 20
    db_name           = "appdb"
    username          = "dbadmin"
    password          = var.db_password
    skip_final_snapshot = true
    publicly_accessible = false

    vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds.id]
    db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
  }
  ```
- **S3 bucket with versioning:**
  ```hcl
  resource "aws_s3_bucket" "assets" {
    bucket = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-assets"
  }

  resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "assets" {
    bucket = aws_s3_bucket.assets.id
    versioning_configuration { status = "Enabled" }
  }
  ```

### 8. Define outputs (`outputs.tf`)
  ```hcl
  output "vpc_id" {
    description = "VPC ID"
    value       = aws_vpc.main.id
  }

  output "db_endpoint" {
    description = "RDS connection endpoint"
    value       = aws_db_instance.postgres.endpoint
    sensitive   = true
  }

  output "s3_bucket_name" {
    description = "Assets S3 bucket name"
    value       = aws_s3_bucket.assets.bucket
  }
  ```
- Retrieve outputs any time with `terraform output`.
- Use `sensitive = true` for outputs that contain secrets (DB endpoints, IPs of private resources).

### 9. Core Terraform workflow
  ```bash
  terraform init       # download providers, configure backend
  terraform validate   # check syntax and config correctness
  terraform fmt        # format .tf files (run before every commit)
  terraform plan       # preview changes — ALWAYS review before applying
  terraform apply      # create/update resources (prompts for confirmation)
  terraform destroy    # tear down all resources (destructive!)
  ```
- ALWAYS run `terraform plan` before `terraform apply`. Never `apply` blindly.
- Save the plan to a file for CI/CD: `terraform plan -out=tfplan && terraform apply tfplan`.

### 10. Workspace strategy for multiple environments
  ```bash
  terraform workspace new staging
  terraform workspace new production
  terraform workspace select staging
  terraform workspace list
  ```
- Use workspaces to manage dev/staging/production state separately in the same backend.
- Reference the workspace in resources:
  ```hcl
  resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
    identifier = "${var.project_name}-${terraform.workspace}-db"
    # ... production gets larger instance
    instance_class = terraform.workspace == "production" ? "db.t3.medium" : "db.t3.micro"
  }
  ```

### 11. CI/CD integration (GitHub Actions)
  ```yaml
  name: Terraform
  on:
    pull_request:
      paths: ['infra/**']
    push:
      branches: [main]
      paths: ['infra/**']

  jobs:
    terraform:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      defaults:
        run:
          working-directory: infra
      steps:
        - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
          with:
            terraform_version: '1.7.0'
        - name: Configure AWS credentials
          uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
          with:
            aws-access-key-id: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
            aws-secret-access-key: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
            aws-region: us-east-1
        - run: terraform init
        - run: terraform validate
        - run: terraform plan -var="db_password=${{ secrets.DB_PASSWORD }}"
          if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        - run: terraform apply -auto-approve -var="db_password=${{ secrets.DB_PASSWORD }}"
          if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
  ```
- Store secrets in GitHub repository secrets, not in `.tf` files.

### 12. Verify the setup
- Run `terraform init` — should output "Terraform has been successfully initialized".
- Run `terraform validate` — should output "Success! The configuration is valid".
- Run `terraform plan` — review the plan output carefully. Expect `+ create` for new resources.
- Run `terraform apply` — type `yes` to confirm. Check the AWS console to verify resources are created.
- Run `terraform output` — confirm outputs are populated with correct values.

### Common Errors & Fixes
- **`Error: No valid credential sources found`** — AWS credentials are not configured. Run `aws configure` or set `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`/`AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` environment variables.
- **`Error: Backend configuration changed`** — Backend config changed after init. Run `terraform init -reconfigure`.
- **`Error: state lock`** — A previous `apply` failed and left a DynamoDB lock. Check the lock table and delete the item manually if the process is no longer running.
- **`Error: InvalidClientTokenId`** — Wrong AWS region in credentials or provider. Check `aws_region` variable and `~/.aws/credentials`.
- **`Error: BucketAlreadyOwnedByYou`** — The S3 state bucket already exists in your account. Import it or use a different name.
- **Plan shows unexpected resource replacement** — A required field that forces replacement (like `availability_zone` on a subnet) changed. Review the plan carefully — replacement destroys the old resource.
- **`Error: Missing required argument`** — A variable has no default and no value in `terraform.tfvars`. Add it to `terraform.tfvars`.

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT run `terraform apply` without reviewing `terraform plan` first — always check what Terraform will change before applying.
- Do NOT store secrets in `.tf` files or commit `terraform.tfvars` — use environment variables or a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault).
- Do NOT use local state (`terraform.tfstate`) for team projects — always configure a remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB).
- Do NOT manually edit the `.tfstate` file — use `terraform state` commands (`terraform state mv`, `terraform state rm`, `terraform import`) for state surgery.
- Do NOT delete the `.terraform.lock.hcl` file from version control — it ensures all team members and CI use the same provider versions.
- Do NOT use `terraform destroy` in production without a full backup and stakeholder approval — it deletes all managed resources.
- Do NOT hardcode AWS account IDs, region names, or AMI IDs in resources — use variables and `data` sources to fetch dynamic values.
- Do NOT grant Terraform's IAM role admin access — follow least-privilege and grant only the permissions needed for the resources being managed.
